title: Praxis Early Childhood Education (5025) - 200 Source Questions + 100 Independent Practice Questions + 200 Guided Review
description: Contains 200 original source questions, 100 assistant-authored independent practice questions, and 200 source-derived guided-review activities. The assistant audit found 205 distinct independent content kernels. Two 100-item diagnostics and an exact 120-item simulation spanning language and literacy, mathematics, social studies, science, health, physical education, and the arts.
contentReview: Assistant audit completed: 300 independent-practice items contain 205 distinct content kernels and 95 parallel variants under the normalized test. 200 additional activities are guided reasoning transformations, not independent exam questions. The 500-distinct-question target is not met.
simulationNote: The official 5025 test currently contains 120 selected-response questions in 120 minutes and does not permit a calculator. This simulation uses the exact 36/30/17/17/20 category counts and original single-choice items.
disclaimer: Independent preparation material, not affiliated with or endorsed by ETS, NAEYC, NGSS, NCSS, SHAPE America, or the National Core Arts Standards. Results are not official forms, scaled scores, pass predictions, licenses, developmental screenings, diagnoses, disability or accommodation decisions, health advice, or substitutes for current state, program, testing, safety, accessibility, and educational requirements.

domains.label: Health and Physical Education; Creative and Performing Arts

items.prompt: In an early-childhood language and literacy context, in a K–2 literacy lesson, how should an elementary literacy teacher use a developmental literacy continuum?
choices: Use the continuum to anticipate relationships and prerequisites while assessing the individual learner rather than treating stages as fixed gates.
Require every learner to master skills at the same age.
Skip foundational evidence when comprehension is weak.
Use grade placement as the only indicator of instructional need.
items.rationale: Literacy components develop in related and partly overlapping ways; a continuum organizes assessment and instruction but does not erase individual, linguistic, cultural, disability, or opportunity differences. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: Correct. Literacy components develop in related and partly overlapping ways; a continuum organizes assessment and instruction but does not erase individual, linguistic, cultural, disability, or opportunity differences. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

items.prompt: In an early-childhood language and literacy context, in a K–2 literacy lesson, which relationship is central to reading comprehension?
choices: Comprehension depends only on reading rate.

items.rationale: Word recognition and language comprehension make distinct, interacting contributions; instructional planning should locate the learner’s relative strengths and needs rather than infer one from the other. items.prompt: In an early-childhood language and literacy context, in a K–2 literacy lesson, which pattern is commonly associated with dyslexia?

items.prompt: In an early-childhood language and literacy context, in a K–2 literacy lesson, how should culture and language influence literacy instruction?

items.prompt: In an early-childhood language and literacy context, while planning explicit emergent-literacy instruction, how do phonological awareness and phonemic awareness differ?

items.prompt: In an early-childhood language and literacy context, while planning explicit emergent-literacy instruction, what is the alphabetic principle?

items.prompt: Without using a calculator, solve or interpret this early-childhood content-knowledge problem: A student has $50 and buys 3 identical notebooks. After the purchase, $32 remains. Which equation can be used to find the price n of one notebook?

items.prompt: Without using a calculator, solve or interpret this early-childhood content-knowledge problem: A student says 1/3 + 1/4 = 2/7. Which response best addresses the error?

items.prompt: Which statement accurately compares two ways that groups establish expectations?
choices: A teacher-created expectation and a municipal requirement both carry the same legal authority and court enforcement.
A group agreement cannot be revised after adoption, whereas a government requirement changes whenever one person objects.
A classroom agreement guides a school community, while an ordinance is enacted by authorized local government and has legal consequences.
A government requirement is legitimate only when every affected person voted for it, while a class agreement needs no participation.
items.rationale: Classroom agreements and laws can both organize shared life, but their authority differs. A classroom agreement is established within a school community; a city ordinance is enacted under governmental authority and carries legally established consequences.
choiceRationales: This confuses a local instructional expectation with a law. Classroom agreements may carry school consequences, but they do not have the legal status or court enforcement of municipal ordinances.
Both classroom agreements and laws can be revised through appropriate processes. Individual objection alone does not automatically change a law, and adoption does not make an agreement permanent.
Correct. The statement distinguishes a school-community agreement from an enactment of authorized local government while recognizing that each can structure conduct in its own setting.

Legitimacy does not require a unanimous vote by every affected person. Laws derive authority from constitutional and legislative processes, while sound classroom agreements invite meaningful participation.

items.prompt: A child says, “The ice probably melted because the tray was near the sunny window.” How should the teacher classify the statement?
choices: It is a direct observation because the child used a complete sentence to report what happened.
It is an inference because the child used observed melting and location information to propose an explanation.
It is a measurement because the child compared the tray with the window without using a number.
It is a controlled conclusion because one observation rules out other possible causes of the melting.
items.rationale: The child interprets observations to propose a cause, so the statement is an inference. An observation would report what senses or tools detected, such as liquid water in the tray; a supported causal conclusion would require additional evidence.
choiceRationales: Sentence form does not determine whether a claim is an observation. The causal word “because” shows that the child is interpreting evidence rather than merely reporting sensed information.
Correct. The child combines observable information about melting and location with prior knowledge about sunlight to offer a tentative explanation, which is an inference.
A qualitative comparison can be evidence, but calling the location relevant does not itself constitute measurement. The statement proposes a cause from observations.
A single uncontrolled observation cannot rule out room temperature, elapsed time, or other causes. The statement is a plausible inference, not a conclusive experiment.

items.prompt: A child says an adult asked them to keep a physical interaction that felt uncomfortable secret. What should an early-childhood teacher reinforce first?
choices: The child should wait for another incident before telling anyone, so the first report can be compared with later events.
The child should decide whether the adult intended harm before seeking help from a trusted person at school or home.
The child should confront the adult alone and request an explanation before involving another responsible adult.
The child can say no, move away when possible, and tell a trusted adult; unsafe touch secrets do not need to be kept.
items.rationale: Developmentally appropriate safety instruction emphasizes bodily autonomy, immediate help seeking, and freedom from blame. A child should tell a trusted adult about unsafe or uncomfortable contact, even when someone labels it a secret.
choiceRationales: Waiting can leave the child without needed protection and support. A report of uncomfortable or unsafe contact should be taken seriously and addressed through current safeguarding procedures.
A child is not responsible for judging an adult’s intent before asking for help. Discomfort or a boundary violation is enough reason to contact a trusted adult.
Requiring a child to confront an adult alone can increase risk and improperly shifts responsibility to the child. Trusted-adult support should be available promptly.
Correct. This teaches bodily autonomy and a concrete help-seeking sequence without blaming the child or requiring secrecy about conduct that feels unsafe or uncomfortable.

items.prompt: A collage includes rough paper, smooth foil, dark blue shapes, and pale yellow lines. Which analysis uses visual-art vocabulary accurately?
choices: The rough and smooth surfaces create texture, while the light and dark qualities of colors create differences in value.
The rough and smooth surfaces create melody, while the blue and yellow areas establish the work’s dramatic setting.

The blue and yellow areas create meter, while the surface differences show the monetary value of each material.
items.rationale: Texture describes actual or implied surface quality, and value describes relative lightness or darkness. Melody, tempo, and meter are music concepts; a flat enclosed area is shape rather than three-dimensional form.
choiceRationales: Correct. The response identifies surface quality as texture and relative lightness or darkness as value, applying each visual-art element to evidence in the collage.
Melody is an ordered sequence of pitches, not a surface quality. Dramatic setting belongs to theater analysis and does not name the color relationship described.
Tempo describes musical speed rather than lightness or darkness. A flat colored area is a shape; form refers to actual or represented three-dimensional volume.
Meter organizes musical beats, and artistic value in this context means lightness or darkness rather than the material’s purchase price.

items.prompt: A teacher plans to introduce a textile tradition from a particular community. Which preparation best supports accurate cultural context?

items.rationale: Culturally responsive arts instruction locates a work in a specific time, community, purpose, and maker perspective. Credible community-connected sources and attention to variation reduce stereotyping and tokenism.

Correct. Community-connected sourcing, specific historical and functional context, and recognition of variation support accurate study without reducing a tradition to a token example.
Formal visual analysis is useful but cannot replace cultural and historical context. Omitting makers and purposes strips the work of information needed for accurate interpretation.
Educational use does not erase community meaning or permissions. Sacred or restricted designs require careful consultation, context, and respect rather than automatic copying.

items.prompt: During a nature walk, a child says, “These tracks may belong to a rabbit.” Which teacher response uses science terms accurately?

items.prompt: Which message best supports children’s body-safety knowledge during a developmentally appropriate lesson?
choices: Children should first determine whether a person meant to cause harm before they describe an uncomfortable interaction.
Children should reserve safety reports for encounters with unfamiliar people because familiar adults can be presumed safe.
Children should solve a boundary concern privately with the person involved before asking another adult for assistance.

items.rationale: Body-safety education should give children clear, usable actions: recognize discomfort, assert a boundary when possible, leave, and seek help from trusted adults. The guidance applies regardless of whether the other person is familiar.
choiceRationales: Children do not need to evaluate another person’s intent before reporting discomfort. That requirement can delay help and place an adult responsibility on the child.

Private confrontation can expose a child to added pressure or risk. Children should have immediate access to responsible, trusted adults who can provide protection and follow procedures.
Correct. The message combines bodily autonomy with persistent, concrete help seeking and does not restrict safety support according to a person’s familiarity.

items.prompt: Children examine a sculpture with curved edges, hollow spaces, and a bumpy surface. Which description is most precise?
choices: The work combines three-dimensional form, negative space, and tactile texture to create visual relationships.
The work combines musical dynamics, dramatic conflict, and tempo because its surface changes across the object.
The work consists only of flat shapes because curved edges prevent an object from occupying three-dimensional space.
The work’s negative space identifies its purchase price, while its texture determines the artist’s cultural identity.
items.rationale: A sculpture occupies three-dimensional space and therefore has form. Open or hollow areas can function as negative space, and a physically bumpy surface has tactile texture.
choiceRationales: Correct. Form names the sculpture’s three-dimensional volume, negative space identifies its openings, and tactile texture describes a surface that can be felt.
Dynamics and tempo are music terms, while dramatic conflict is a theater concept. None accurately identifies the sculpture’s volume, openings, or surface quality.
Curved edges do not make an object flat. A sculpture has height, width, and depth, so three-dimensional form is the appropriate element.
Negative space refers to unoccupied areas around or within forms, not cost. Surface texture alone cannot establish an artist’s cultural identity.

This option applies the claim "Ask the student to round both numbers to the nearest whole number only."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that the misconception concerns positional value.

items.prompt: For a parallel early-childhood language and literacy decision, when an elementary teacher plans word-analysis instruction, a child can blend /m/ /a/ /p/ but cannot segment the word map. What is the clearest next instructional target?
choices: Advanced figurative language.
Explicit supported phoneme segmentation linked to the letters once the oral task is understood.
Silent-reading endurance.
Dictionary guide words.
items.rationale: The contrast between blending and segmentation identifies a specific sound-analysis need that can be taught directly and connected to encoding and decoding. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: This option applies the claim "Advanced figurative language."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that the contrast between blending and segmentation identifies a specific sound-analysis need that can be taught directly and connected to encoding and decoding.

Correct. The contrast between blending and segmentation identifies a specific sound-analysis need that can be taught directly and connected to encoding and decoding. items.prompt: A beginning early-childhood teacher reviews this parallel content problem: Students need to compare category totals across four months. Which graph is generally the clearest choice?
choices: A circle graph for each individual data point
An unlabeled line with months placed in random order
A grouped bar graph with consistent category colors
A pictograph that changes the symbol value each month
items.rationale: The goal is to compare several categories repeatedly across months. Grouped bars support direct comparisons within and across months when labels, colors, and scale remain consistent.
choiceRationales: This option applies the claim "A circle graph for each individual data point"; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that the goal is to compare several categories repeatedly across months.

This option applies the claim "An unlabeled line with months placed in random order"; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that the goal is to compare several categories repeatedly across months.
Correct. The goal is to compare several categories repeatedly across months. Grouped bars support direct comparisons within and across months when labels, colors, and scale remain consistent.

This option applies the claim "A pictograph that changes the symbol value each month"; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that the goal is to compare several categories repeatedly across months.

items.prompt: For a parallel early-childhood language and literacy decision, when an elementary teacher plans word-analysis instruction, a student reads memorized words well but struggles with novel letter strings. What does a controlled unfamiliar-word task help assess?
choices: Background knowledge of the story.
Writing organization.
Application of phoneme–grapheme and word-analysis knowledge without relying only on stored word familiarity.
Listening comprehension only.
items.rationale: Novel-word tasks can sample generative decoding, but interpretation still requires appropriate language, pattern legality, instruction, and comparison with real-word and spelling evidence. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: This option applies the claim "Background knowledge of the story."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that novel-word tasks can sample generative decoding, but interpretation still requires appropriate language, pattern legality, instruction, and comparison with real-word and spelling evidence.

This option applies the claim "Writing organization."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that novel-word tasks can sample generative decoding, but interpretation still requires appropriate language, pattern legality, instruction, and comparison with real-word and spelling evidence.

This option applies the claim "Listening comprehension only."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that novel-word tasks can sample generative decoding, but interpretation still requires appropriate language, pattern legality, instruction, and comparison with real-word and spelling evidence.

items.prompt: For a parallel early-childhood language and literacy decision, when an elementary teacher plans word-analysis instruction, a teacher collects oral reading, retelling, and question responses across graded passages. How should the information be used?
choices: As a single standardized diagnosis.
As proof of an exact independent grade level.
As a replacement for word-level analysis.
As descriptive task evidence about accuracy, fluency, and comprehension, integrated with more reliable measures and authentic performance.
items.rationale: Passage inventories reveal strategies and errors in context but their level labels and comprehension scores can be unstable; decisions need converging evidence. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: This option applies the claim "As a single standardized diagnosis."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that passage inventories reveal strategies and errors in context but their level labels and comprehension scores can be unstable; decisions need converging evidence.

This option applies the claim "As proof of an exact independent grade level."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that passage inventories reveal strategies and errors in context but their level labels and comprehension scores can be unstable; decisions need converging evidence.

items.prompt: For a parallel early-childhood language and literacy decision, when an elementary teacher plans word-analysis instruction, a learner spells “jumped” as “jumpt” and “running” as “runing.” What is most useful?
choices: Examine phoneme representation, orthographic conventions, inflectional morphology, and doubling patterns rather than count errors only.
Treat both words as random visual failures.
Assess comprehension instead of spelling.
Correct the words without identifying patterns.
items.rationale: Feature analysis shows what the learner represents and which phonological, orthographic, syllabic, or morphological patterns need explicit instruction. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: Correct. Feature analysis shows what the learner represents and which phonological, orthographic, syllabic, or morphological patterns need explicit instruction. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

This option applies the claim "Treat both words as random visual failures."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that feature analysis shows what the learner represents and which phonological, orthographic, syllabic, or morphological patterns need explicit instruction.

This option applies the claim "Correct the words without identifying patterns."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that feature analysis shows what the learner represents and which phonological, orthographic, syllabic, or morphological patterns need explicit instruction.

items.prompt: For a parallel early-childhood language and literacy decision, when an elementary teacher plans word-analysis instruction, a student reads many words correctly per minute but ignores punctuation and cannot explain the passage. What conclusion is warranted?
choices: The student is fully fluent because rate is high.
Rate is one indicator; accuracy, prosody, text conditions, language, and comprehension show that fluent meaningful reading is not yet established.
The passage was understood because few words were missed.
Prosody is unrelated to syntax or meaning.
items.rationale: A fluency decision integrates rate with accuracy, phrasing, expression, difficulty, and understanding rather than using a speed cutoff in isolation. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.
choiceRationales: This option applies the claim "The student is fully fluent because rate is high."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that a fluency decision integrates rate with accuracy, phrasing, expression, difficulty, and understanding rather than using a speed cutoff in isolation.

Correct. A fluency decision integrates rate with accuracy, phrasing, expression, difficulty, and understanding rather than using a speed cutoff in isolation. The decision should remain tied to the assessed literacy skill, grade-appropriate text access, explicit instruction, and observable progress evidence.

This option applies the claim "The passage was understood because few words were missed."; it conflicts with the item-specific principle that a fluency decision integrates rate with accuracy, phrasing, expression, difficulty, and understanding rather than using a speed cutoff in isolation.

items.prompt: During an interactive read-aloud, several children answer with one-word responses. Which teacher move most directly expands oral language?
choices: Pause after an open question, invite a child to explain, and recast the response in a slightly more complex sentence.
Ask only yes-or-no questions so every response can be scored quickly.
Supply the complete answer before children have time to formulate language.
Correct each grammatical difference without responding to the child’s meaning.
items.rationale: Open prompts, wait time, elaboration, and responsive recasts give children meaningful opportunities to produce and hear language just beyond their current forms.
choiceRationales: Correct. Open prompts, wait time, elaboration, and responsive recasts give children meaningful opportunities to produce and hear language just beyond their current forms.
This response reduces opportunities to formulate connected language because the response form is limited in advance.
This response removes productive language practice and prevents the teacher from learning what children can express independently.
This response treats language variation as error and can interrupt communication instead of extending meaning and form.

items.prompt: A child points to a picture of a dog after the teacher says, “Show me dog,” but does not yet say the word. What does this performance demonstrate?
choices: Expressive vocabulary is fully mastered because pointing and speaking are interchangeable responses.
Receptive understanding of the word is evident even though expressive production is not yet demonstrated.
Print decoding is established because the child located an object after hearing its spoken name.
Phoneme segmentation is established because the child connected one spoken word with one referent.
items.rationale: Following the spoken direction shows receptive vocabulary. Expressive knowledge would require producing the word, and decoding would require interpreting printed language.
choiceRationales: This response overstates the evidence because recognizing a spoken word does not show that the child can retrieve and pronounce it.
Correct. Following the spoken direction shows receptive vocabulary. Expressive knowledge would require producing the word, and decoding would require interpreting printed language.
This response confuses listening comprehension with reading; no printed word was presented for the child to decode.
This response misidentifies a meaning task as phonological analysis because the child did not separate any word into sounds.

items.prompt: A preschooler writes a row of letterlike forms and says, “This says my dog runs.” What is the most appropriate teacher response?
choices: Erase the forms and require the child to copy the conventional sentence before discussing the message.
Label the work as drawing because letterlike forms cannot communicate until spelling is conventional.
Ask the child to read the message, add a conventional transcription nearby, and invite more details without replacing the child’s marks.
Delay writing experiences until the child can name and form every uppercase and lowercase letter.
items.rationale: The marks show emergent understanding that writing carries a message. Valuing the child’s reading while modeling conventional print extends meaning, print concepts, and authorship.
choiceRationales: This response substitutes copying for composition and can obscure evidence of the child’s current symbolic and language knowledge.
This response dismisses an important emergent-writing behavior: intentional marks can represent a stable message before conventional spelling.
Correct. The marks show emergent understanding that writing carries a message. Valuing the child’s reading while modeling conventional print extends meaning, print concepts, and authorship.
This response unnecessarily withholds authentic composition while foundational letter knowledge is still developing through use.

items.prompt: A multilingual child tells a detailed story in the home language but gives brief answers in English. Which interpretation is best supported?
choices: The brief English responses prove that the child lacks narrative structure in every language.
Use of the home language is interfering with literacy and should be excluded during assessment.
The English sample alone is sufficient because language knowledge transfers only after complete English fluency.
The child’s narrative knowledge may exceed current English expression, so assessment should include both languages and multiple response modes.
items.rationale: Language proficiency affects how knowledge is displayed. Evidence from the home language, interpreters or family input, and supported English tasks helps distinguish conceptual skill from English production.
choiceRationales: This response generalizes from limited English output and ignores direct evidence of organized narrative performance in another language.
This response treats bilingualism as a deficit and removes a valid source of evidence about language and conceptual knowledge.
This response assumes one sample captures the construct even though the response language constrains what the child can demonstrate.

Correct. Language proficiency affects how knowledge is displayed. Evidence from the home language, interpreters or family input, and supported English tasks helps distinguish conceptual skill from English production.

items.prompt: Children are learning the word enormous during a read-aloud. Which explanation is most instructionally useful?
choices: “Enormous means very, very large; this elephant is enormous compared with the mouse. Show me something else enormous.”
“Enormous means enormous; remember it when the word appears again.”
“Enormous is an adjective with four syllables, so its meaning is not important yet.”
“Look at the first letter and guess a word that would fit the page.”
items.rationale: A child-friendly definition, contextual contrast, and prompt for active use connect pronunciation, meaning, and application more effectively than exposure alone.
choiceRationales: Correct. A child-friendly definition, contextual contrast, and prompt for active use connect pronunciation, meaning, and application more effectively than exposure alone.
This response provides no new semantic information and does not let children connect the word with an example or use it.
This response offers form information but wrongly withholds the meaning needed for comprehension and expressive vocabulary.
This response uses a print-guessing routine that cannot establish the meaning of an unfamiliar spoken word.

items.prompt: A teacher wants children to understand how a spoken message becomes a written one. Which activity best targets that goal?
choices: Display an alphabet chart and ask children to name colors around its border.
Write a child’s dictated sentence while rereading each word and tracking the print from left to right.
Have children memorize the page number on which a favorite illustration appears.
Ask children to sort books by cover size without opening or discussing them.
items.rationale: Shared dictation visibly connects a child’s spoken message with written words, directionality, spacing, and rereading while preserving authentic meaning.
choiceRationales: This response focuses attention on a decorative feature rather than the relationship among speech, words, and print.
Correct. Shared dictation visibly connects a child’s spoken message with written words, directionality, spacing, and rereading while preserving authentic meaning.
This response builds location memory but does not demonstrate how written symbols encode an oral message.
This response uses a physical attribute of books and provides no evidence about print carrying language.

items.prompt: In a story retell, a child names the characters and ending but omits the problem and attempts to solve it. What should the teacher probe next?
choices: Ask the child to recite the author’s biography and the book’s publication date.
Ask the child to list every color used in the illustrations in alphabetical order.
Ask what the character wanted, what got in the way, what the character tried, and how the situation was resolved.
Ask whether the child liked the story and end the retell assessment after a yes-or-no answer.
items.rationale: Targeted prompts about goal, problem, and attempts reveal understanding of causal story structure and support a more coherent retell without supplying the events.
choiceRationales: This response requests background facts that do not clarify the missing causal structure in the child’s retell.
This response directs attention to incidental visual details rather than the relationship among problem, actions, and resolution.
Correct. Targeted prompts about goal, problem, and attempts reveal understanding of causal story structure and support a more coherent retell without supplying the events.
This response collects a preference but does not probe the narrative elements that were absent from the performance.

items.prompt: A family uses American Sign Language at home. Which classroom practice best treats the family’s language as an instructional asset?
choices: Ask the family to stop signing so spoken English becomes the child’s only communication model.
Place the child behind peers during group discussion so movement does not distract other children.
Count signed ideas as motor imitation rather than language when documenting progress.
Learn and use key signs with family guidance, provide visual access to communication, and include signed responses in language evidence.
items.rationale: ASL is a complete natural language. Collaboration, visual access, and recognition of signed communication support participation and valid language assessment.
choiceRationales: This response devalues the home language and removes accessible communication rather than building additional language resources.
This response blocks sight lines that are necessary for signed language, facial grammar, and shared attention.
This response misclassifies meaningful linguistic production and would produce invalid evidence about the child’s language abilities.
Correct. ASL is a complete natural language. Collaboration, visual access, and recognition of signed communication support participation and valid language assessment.

items.prompt: Which child response correctly segments the spoken word sunset into syllables?
choices: sun / set
s / u / n / s / e / t
su / nse / t
sunset / sun
items.rationale: Syllable segmentation divides a spoken word into pronounceable beats. Sunset contains two syllables, /sun/ and /set/, rather than six phonemes or arbitrary letter groups.
choiceRationales: Correct. Syllable segmentation divides a spoken word into pronounceable beats. Sunset contains two syllables, /sun/ and /set/, rather than six phonemes or arbitrary letter groups.
This response represents individual letters or phonemes rather than the two larger spoken syllable units.
This response creates divisions that do not correspond to natural spoken beats in the word.
This response repeats whole-word material instead of partitioning the spoken word into its component syllables.

items.prompt: A child can identify rhyming words but cannot blend onset and rime. Which prompt directly assesses onset-rime blending?
choices: “Do map and sun rhyme?”
“What word do /m/ and /ap/ make when you put them together?”
“How many syllables are in map?”
“What is the last phoneme in map?”
items.rationale: The prompt separates the initial consonant onset /m/ from the rime /ap/ and asks the child to combine them into the spoken word map.
choiceRationales: This response checks rhyme recognition but does not require the child to synthesize an onset with a rime.
Correct. The prompt separates the initial consonant onset /m/ from the rime /ap/ and asks the child to combine them into the spoken word map.
This response targets syllable counting, a larger-unit segmentation task rather than onset-rime blending.
This response asks for phoneme isolation and does not require combining two spoken parts.

items.prompt: A teacher asks, “What is the first sound in fish?” Which response shows phoneme isolation rather than letter naming?
choices: The child says “eff.”
The child says “fish has four letters.”
The child says /f/.
The child says “fish rhymes with dish.”
items.rationale: Phoneme isolation requires producing the speech sound /f/ at the beginning of fish. Saying a letter name or describing spelling and rhyme demonstrates different knowledge.
choiceRationales: This response provides the alphabetic letter name rather than isolating the consonant sound heard in the word.
This response reports orthographic length and does not identify any individual speech sound.
Correct. Phoneme isolation requires producing the speech sound /f/ at the beginning of fish. Saying a letter name or describing spelling and rhyme demonstrates different knowledge.
This response shows rhyme awareness at the word ending rather than isolation of the initial phoneme.

items.prompt: Which sequence provides the clearest scaffold for a child learning to blend /s/ /a/ /t/?
choices: Insert a pause after every phoneme and ask the child to memorize the three separate sounds.
Show a picture and accept any word that begins with /s/ without blending the remaining sounds.
Ask the child to spell a longer rhyming word before combining the three target phonemes.
Have the child hold /s/, connect it to /a/, add /t/, and then say sat without pauses.
items.rationale: Continuous blending reduces memory demands by connecting sounds across the word and culminates in pronunciation of the whole word sat.
choiceRationales: This response maintains separation among sounds and does not model the coarticulation needed to recognize the whole word.
This response permits guessing from the initial sound and picture rather than attending to the complete sound sequence.
This response adds unnecessary spelling complexity before the child has practiced blending the target sequence.
Correct. Continuous blending reduces memory demands by connecting sounds across the word and culminates in pronunciation of the whole word sat.

items.prompt: A child can say smile but cannot say the word after hearing /s/ /m/ /ī/ /l/. What does the error most directly indicate?
choices: The child needs practice blending a four-phoneme sequence into the complete spoken form.
The child necessarily lacks the vocabulary meaning of smile.
The child has demonstrated mastery of phoneme deletion.
The child needs to identify the number of printed letters before hearing sounds.
items.rationale: Producing the known word but failing to synthesize its separately presented phonemes points to phoneme blending, not necessarily vocabulary or print knowledge.
choiceRationales: Correct. Producing the known word but failing to synthesize its separately presented phonemes points to phoneme blending, not necessarily vocabulary or print knowledge.
This response infers a semantic deficit despite evidence that the child can already produce the meaningful spoken word.
This response names a different manipulation skill; no phoneme was removed from a word in the task.
This response introduces orthographic counting even though the assessed task operates on a sequence of speech sounds.

items.prompt: When asked to say plane without /p/, a child answers pane. How should the response be scored?
choices: Correct; the letters p and l must both be removed because they form one onset.
Incorrect; deleting /p/ from /p/ /l/ /ā/ /n/ yields lane.
Correct; pane preserves the long-vowel spelling pattern from the original word.
Incorrect; the target should be lain because phoneme deletion always changes spelling but not pronunciation.
items.rationale: The spoken word plane begins with the phoneme sequence /p/ /l/. Removing only /p/ leaves /l/ /ā/ /n/, pronounced lane. The child's answer changes the remaining onset and is not the requested deletion.
choiceRationales: This response removes two phonemes even though the direction identifies only the initial /p/ sound.
Correct. The spoken word plane begins with the phoneme sequence /p/ /l/. Removing only /p/ leaves /l/ /ā/ /n/, pronounced lane.
This response uses visual spelling as the criterion even though phoneme deletion is performed on spoken sounds.
This response misrepresents the resulting pronunciation and makes an unsupported generalization about spelling changes.

items.prompt: A kindergarten class has learned /m/, /s/, /t/, /a/, and /p/. Which new practice word is fully decodable from those correspondences?
choices: ship
goat
map
cheese
items.rationale: Map uses only the taught correspondences /m/ /a/ /p/. The other words require digraphs or vowel patterns that are not included in the stated instruction.
choiceRationales: This response requires the digraph sh and the vowel i, neither of which appears in the taught set.
This response requires the vowel o and an advanced vowel spelling pattern rather than only the listed correspondences.
Correct. Map uses only the taught correspondences /m/ /a/ /p/. The other words require digraphs or vowel patterns that are not included in the stated instruction.
This response requires the digraph ch and a vowel-team pattern that have not been introduced.

items.prompt: A child reads sat as /s/…/a/…/t/ but does not recognize the whole word. What is the best immediate prompt?
choices: “Ignore the letters and name something in the picture that might fit.”
“Say each letter name three times and move to the next sentence.”
“Memorize the outline of sat so you will not need its sounds.”
“Blend it again without stopping between sounds: /sssaaat/, sat.”
items.rationale: The child has accessed the correspondences but needs support connecting them continuously and pronouncing the synthesized word. A brief continuous-blending model addresses that need.
choiceRationales: This response shifts from complete orthographic information to contextual guessing and may reinforce inaccurate word reading.
This response practices letter names rather than synthesizing the phonemes the child already produced.
This response encourages visual memorization and bypasses the alphabetic process the error shows is nearly established.
Correct. The child has accessed the correspondences but needs support connecting them continuously and pronouncing the synthesized word. A brief continuous-blending model addresses that need.

items.prompt: Which word begins with the consonant digraph /sh/?
choices: shell
sale
cell
chair
items.rationale: In shell, the two letters sh represent the single initial phoneme /sh/. Sale begins /s/, cell begins /s/, and chair begins with /ch/.
choiceRationales: Correct. In shell, the two letters sh represent the single initial phoneme /sh/. Sale begins /s/, cell begins /s/, and chair begins with /ch/.
This response begins with the single phoneme /s/ represented by one initial consonant letter.
This response also begins /s/ even though the letter c represents that sound in this word.
This response begins with the affricate /ch/, represented by a different consonant digraph.

items.prompt: A child reads pet as pit. Which knowledge should the teacher check first?
choices: Whether the child knows that every final consonant changes the preceding vowel to long.
Whether the child distinguishes the medial short-e and short-i sounds and connects them to e and i.
Whether the child can use the illustration to replace the printed word with a plausible noun.
Whether the child has memorized the sentence in which pet appeared.
items.rationale: The substitution preserves the initial and final consonants but changes the medial vowel, so the most diagnostic next step is comparing /e/ and /i/ and their graphemes.
choiceRationales: This response states a false generalization; a final consonant in a closed syllable commonly supports a short vowel.
Correct. The substitution preserves the initial and final consonants but changes the medial vowel, so the most diagnostic next step is comparing /e/ and /i/ and their graphemes.
This response encourages contextual substitution instead of identifying the specific vowel correspondence causing the error.
This response would mask rather than analyze the child’s use of the medial grapheme.
referenceNames: Early Childhood Education (5025) — Official ETS Study Companion

items.prompt: Which routine best teaches the irregular high-frequency word said while preserving phonics knowledge?
choices: Ask children to memorize said as an unanalyzed picture without attending to any letters or sounds.
Teach that ai always represents /e/ so children can apply the rule to every unfamiliar word.
Map /s/ to s and /d/ to d, identify ai as the unexpected spelling for /e/, and practice reading and writing the whole word.
Have children guess said from a picture and the first letter whenever it appears.
items.rationale: An irregular word still contains regular grapheme-phoneme relationships. Explicitly mapping the regular and unexpected parts supports orthographic learning without inventing a false rule.
choiceRationales: This response discards useful alphabetic information and makes retention depend on undifferentiated visual memory.
This response overgeneralizes an exception and would lead children to misread many regular ai words.
Correct. An irregular word still contains regular grapheme-phoneme relationships. Explicitly mapping the regular and unexpected parts supports orthographic learning without inventing a false rule.
This response uses partial visual and contextual cues rather than building a complete representation of the printed word.
referenceNames: Early Childhood Education (5025) — Official ETS Study Companion

Principles of Child Development and Learning and Implications That Inform Practice — NAEYC

items.prompt: The teacher dictates the word jump. A child writes JUP. Which feedback most directly addresses the encoding evidence?
choices: “The whole word is wrong, so copy jump five times without examining its sounds.”
“Add any letter before p because four-letter words must contain four different sounds.”
“Use the picture to decide which consonant belongs in the middle.”
“You heard /j/, /u/, and /p/. Stretch jump and listen for the sound between /u/ and /p/: /m/.”
items.rationale: JUP represents three of four phonemes in jump and omits /m/. A targeted stretch and phoneme prompt acknowledges correct mappings and directs attention to the missing sound.
choiceRationales: This response fails to use the informative partial spelling and replaces analysis with copying.
This response relies on letter count and an arbitrary addition rather than identifying the omitted phoneme.
This response uses semantic context even though the missing information can be derived from the spoken sound sequence.
Correct. JUP represents three of four phonemes in jump and omits /m/. A targeted stretch and phoneme prompt acknowledges correct mappings and directs attention to the missing sound.

items.prompt: A child reads a passage accurately but word by word with long pauses. Which interpretation is most appropriate?
choices: Decoding is accurate, but automaticity and phrasing need support through repeated oral reading.
The pauses are enough to conclude that comprehension is weak, so passage questions should replace fluency practice.
Because every word was identified correctly, fluency is adequate and repeated oral reading would add little.
The passage is probably too easy, so increasing text complexity is the best next step for smoother reading.
items.rationale: Fluency includes accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression. Accurate but effortful reading indicates that decoding is available while automaticity and phrasing remain appropriate instructional targets.
choiceRationales: Correct. Fluency includes accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression. Accurate but effortful reading indicates that decoding is available while automaticity and phrasing remain appropriate instructional targets.
This response infers weak comprehension from pausing alone even though the sample provides no direct comprehension evidence and shows accurate word identification.
This response treats accuracy as the whole of fluency and overlooks the observed rate and phrasing difficulties that repeated oral reading can address.
This response raises text demand before the child reads the current passage efficiently, which is unlikely to address the identified automaticity need.

items.prompt: In oral reading, a child substitutes house for home and the sentence remains meaningful. What should the teacher examine to understand the cue used?
choices: Score the substitution as acceptable because preserving sentence meaning matters more than matching the specific printed word.
Compare the response with the printed letters; preserved meaning plus a visual mismatch suggests context was favored over full orthographic processing.
Treat the substitution as weak comprehension because using a semantically related word shows that the sentence was misunderstood.
Measure volume and rate on the substituted word to decide whether fluency, rather than word identification, caused the response.
items.rationale: A meaning-preserving substitution suggests that semantic context influenced the response, while the mismatch between house and home reveals incomplete attention to the printed word. Comparing all available orthographic information tests that interpretation.
choiceRationales: This response confuses a semantically acceptable sentence with accurate word identification; the response still fails to match the author’s printed word.

Correct. A meaning-preserving substitution suggests that semantic context influenced the response, while the mismatch between house and home reveals incomplete attention to the printed word. Comparing all available orthographic information tests that interpretation.
This response draws the opposite inference from the evidence because a related substitution shows some access to meaning, not its absence.
This response samples delivery features that do not explain why the child selected a different printed word with related meaning.

items.prompt: Which word contains an inflectional ending that marks past tense?
choices: jumper
jumping
jumped
jumps
items.rationale: The ending -ed in jumped marks past tense. The suffix -er forms a noun, -ing marks a progressive or participial form, and -s can mark present tense agreement.
choiceRationales: This response uses the derivational suffix -er to name a person or thing associated with the action.
This response contains -ing, which does not by itself mark simple past tense.
Correct. The ending -ed in jumped marks past tense. The suffix -er forms a noun, -ing marks a progressive or participial form, and -s can mark present tense agreement.
This response uses -s, which commonly marks third-person singular present tense in this context.

items.prompt: A child can name letters but gives inconsistent sounds when shown individual graphemes. Which assessment adds the most useful evidence?
choices: Present the letters alphabetically and record whether the child can sing each printed letter name in sequence.
Pair each letter with a familiar picture and record whether the child can name the pictured object.
Show uppercase-lowercase pairs and record whether the child gives the same letter name for both forms.
Present the letters in varied order without pictures and record the sound given for each.
items.rationale: Varied grapheme presentation without semantic cues directly samples letter-sound knowledge and reduces support from sequence memory or pictures. Letter-name matching supplies different evidence from the inconsistent sound responses in the stem.
choiceRationales: This response samples rehearsed sequence and letter-name production rather than independent retrieval of the sound represented by each grapheme.
This response measures object naming with a semantic cue, which can hide whether the child knows the grapheme-phoneme correspondence.
This response checks recognition of letter-name identity across cases but does not resolve the reported inconsistency in sound production.

Correct. Varied grapheme presentation without semantic cues directly samples letter-sound knowledge and reduces support from sequence memory or pictures. Letter-name matching supplies different evidence from the inconsistent sound responses in the stem.

items.prompt: After reading that puddles disappeared on a warm afternoon, a child says, “The water went into the air.” What evidence best supports the inference?
choices: The text describes warmer conditions and less standing water even though no one poured or drained it.
The illustration uses blue for both the puddle and the sky.
The page number is larger after the puddle disappears.
The story contains more sentences about boots than about water.
items.rationale: The changing puddle size under warm conditions supports evaporation as an explanation, while the absence of pouring or drainage helps rule out simpler alternatives.
choiceRationales: Correct. The changing puddle size under warm conditions supports evaporation as an explanation, while the absence of pouring or drainage helps rule out simpler alternatives.
This response uses a shared illustration color that does not establish a physical change or causal relationship.
This response treats pagination as evidence about events rather than a navigation feature of the book.
This response compares topic frequency without connecting any fact to the disappearance of the puddle.

items.prompt: Children are retelling a story in which a character repeatedly tries to retrieve a kite. Which organizer best supports causal story structure?

Boxes labeled goal, obstacle, attempts, result, and response
A calendar showing the publication year of several unrelated books
A table sorting illustrations by dominant background color
items.rationale: Goal, obstacle, attempts, result, and response represent connected narrative elements and help children explain why events occur rather than list isolated details.
choiceRationales: This response organizes orthographic features but does not represent relationships among narrative events.
Correct. Goal, obstacle, attempts, result, and response represent connected narrative elements and help children explain why events occur rather than list isolated details.
This response provides bibliographic chronology unrelated to the causal sequence within the target story.
This response classifies visual properties without supporting the character’s problem, actions, and outcome.

items.prompt: Which text feature would most directly help a child locate where monarch butterflies migrate?
choices: A glossary defining chrysalis
A caption describing wing color
A labeled map showing the migration route
An index entry for nectar
items.rationale: A labeled map represents locations and movement across space, directly answering where the butterflies travel. The other features address definitions, appearance, or a different topic.
choiceRationales: This response defines a life-cycle term but does not show the geographic path of migration.
This response adds information about appearance rather than identifying origin, route, or destination.
Correct. A labeled map represents locations and movement across space, directly answering where the butterflies travel. The other features address definitions, appearance, or a different topic.

items.prompt: During a writing conference, a child’s story has clear events but readers cannot tell who is speaking. Which prompt is most useful?
choices: “Replace every spoken line with a longer narration so dialogue is removed.”
“Copy the teacher’s story because published writing should use identical sentences.”
“Check the handwriting first; meaning cannot be revised until every letter is perfect.”
“Where could you add a speaker tag or punctuation so the conversation is easy to follow?”
items.rationale: The prompt identifies a reader need and invites the child to revise dialogue conventions while preserving authorship and the story’s existing strengths.
choiceRationales: This response eliminates a purposeful craft choice instead of helping the writer make that choice comprehensible.
This response replaces composition with imitation and does not address the child’s intended speakers.
This response prioritizes transcription perfection over a revision that directly improves communication and meaning.
Correct. The prompt identifies a reader need and invites the child to revise dialogue conventions while preserving authorship and the story’s existing strengths.

items.prompt: A classroom library includes animal stories only when the characters behave like people. What addition would most broaden children’s literary experience?
choices: Informational texts, traditional tales, poetry, and realistic fiction from varied cultures and perspectives
More copies of the same animal story with different cover colors
Books selected only because every title contains the same number of words
A single folktale presented as representative of all cultures and time periods
items.rationale: A varied collection expands genre knowledge, background knowledge, language forms, and representation. Breadth requires meaningful differences in content and perspective, not cosmetic variation.
choiceRationales: Correct. A varied collection expands genre knowledge, background knowledge, language forms, and representation. Breadth requires meaningful differences in content and perspective, not cosmetic variation.
This response increases access to one title but does not broaden genre, informational content, or represented perspectives.
This response uses an arbitrary surface feature rather than literary quality, developmental access, or meaningful diversity.
This response tokenizes one source and inaccurately treats a specific tradition as universal.

items.prompt: During shared writing, children dictate “We saw three birds” and continue with another sentence. Which teacher think-aloud best models conventions in context?
choices: “Every line needs a comma, so I will place one after each word.”
“Our idea is complete, so I will add a period; the next idea begins with a capital letter.”
“Punctuation is decoration, so any mark will communicate the same relationship.”
“I will wait to discuss marks until children can spell every word conventionally.”
items.rationale: The think-aloud connects sentence boundaries with meaning and visibly models a period and initial capitalization while children compose an authentic message.
choiceRationales: This response teaches a false rule and would obscure rather than clarify phrase and sentence boundaries.
Correct. The think-aloud connects sentence boundaries with meaning and visibly models a period and initial capitalization while children compose an authentic message.
This response misrepresents punctuation as arbitrary even though marks signal specific syntactic and discourse relationships.
This response unnecessarily separates composition from conventions that can be modeled meaningfully during emergent writing.

items.prompt: A child counts eight counters correctly and then says there are eight counters. Which principle is the child demonstrating?
choices: Stable order: number words may be recited in any sequence without changing the count.
Conservation: the set must have the same color before and after counting.
Cardinality: the last number word tells how many objects are in the counted set.
Subitizing: every set of eight can be identified instantly without counting.
items.rationale: Cardinality is the understanding that the final number word in a one-to-one count represents the quantity of the whole set.
choiceRationales: This response misstates stable order, which requires number words to be used in a consistent conventional sequence.
This response confuses quantity conservation with an irrelevant attribute; color does not define the number in a set.
Correct. Cardinality is the understanding that the final number word in a one-to-one count represents the quantity of the whole set.
This response names rapid quantity recognition, but the child intentionally counted each of the eight objects.

items.prompt: A teacher flashes a five-frame showing three dots for two seconds. What response best develops perceptual subitizing?
choices: Leave the frame visible and require children to touch-count each dot repeatedly.
Ask children to name the color of each dot without discussing quantity.
Tell children that all five-frames show five regardless of how many spaces are filled.
Ask, “How many did you see, and how did the arrangement help you know?”
items.rationale: Briefly viewing a small structured set and explaining the quantity supports immediate recognition and connects spatial patterns with number.
choiceRationales: This response changes the task to one-to-one counting and removes the brief visual condition central to perceptual subitizing.
This response focuses on a nonnumerical attribute and provides no evidence about rapid quantity recognition.
This response confuses frame capacity with the number of occupied spaces and teaches an inaccurate quantity rule.
Correct. Briefly viewing a small structured set and explaining the quantity supports immediate recognition and connects spatial patterns with number.

items.prompt: A child says 7 + 5 is 12 because 7 + 3 makes 10 and 2 remain. What strategy did the child use?
choices: Composing a ten and adding the remaining part
Counting backward by the second addend
Doubling seven and subtracting five
Comparing the place values of two two-digit numbers
items.rationale: The child decomposed 5 into 3 and 2, combined 7 and 3 to make the benchmark 10, and then added the remaining 2. This is the make-a-ten strategy.
choiceRationales: Correct. The child decomposed 5 into 3 and 2, combined 7 and 3 to make the benchmark 10, and then added the remaining 2.
This response would involve subtraction rather than decomposing an addend to create a useful benchmark.
This response does not represent the stated steps and would produce a different numerical relationship.
This response is unrelated because the problem contains one-digit addends rather than two-digit place-value comparison.

items.prompt: Which equation represents this situation: Mia had 6 shells, found some more, and then had 11?
items.rationale: The starting amount is 6, an unknown quantity is added, and the resulting total is 11, so the unknown belongs in the addend position.
choiceRationales: This response adds the known total to the starting amount instead of representing the change that produced the total.
Correct. The starting amount is 6, an unknown quantity is added, and the resulting total is 11, so the unknown belongs in the addend position.
This response reverses the quantities in a subtraction expression and does not model shells being added.

items.prompt: Luis has 9 blocks and Ava has 6. Which equation finds how many more blocks Luis has?
items.rationale: A “how many more” comparison asks for the difference between the larger and smaller quantities. Subtracting 6 from 9 gives a difference of 3.
choiceRationales: This response finds the combined total rather than the distance between the two quantities.
This response reverses the subtraction and then assigns a positive result without accounting for the order.
Correct. A “how many more” comparison asks for the difference between the larger and smaller quantities. Subtracting 6 from 9 gives a difference of 3.
This response uses the correct difference as a subtrahend but states an incorrect equality.

items.prompt: Which description correctly represents the number 34?
choices: Three ones and four tens
Thirty tens and four ones
Three tens and fourteen ones as the only possible representation
Three tens and four ones
items.rationale: In base ten, the 3 in the tens place represents 30 and the 4 in the ones place represents 4, for a total of 34. Place value determines each digit's value.
choiceRationales: This response reverses the place values and represents 43 rather than 34.
This response treats the digit 3 as thirty groups of ten, producing 304.
This response ignores equivalent decompositions such as two tens and fourteen ones, so the exclusivity claim is false.
Correct. In base ten, the 3 in the tens place represents 30 and the 4 in the ones place represents 4, for a total of 34.

items.prompt: A child says 8 + 4 = 7 + 5 is false because the numbers on the two sides are different. Which model best addresses the misconception?
choices: Build 12 counters in two groupings and compare the total quantities.
Circle the largest numeral on each side and compare only those two numerals.
Replace the equal sign with an arrow pointing toward the right-hand expression.
Ask the child to memorize that every equation with four addends is true.
items.rationale: The equal sign states that two expressions have the same value. Two arrangements of 12 counters make equivalence visible despite different addends.
choiceRationales: Correct. The equal sign states that two expressions have the same value. Two arrangements of 12 counters make equivalence visible despite different addends.
This response compares isolated addends rather than evaluating the complete expressions on both sides.
This response reinforces the misconception that the symbol signals an action or direction instead of equivalence.
This response substitutes a false rule for reasoning because many expressions with four addends are not equal.

items.prompt: A pattern begins 2, 5, 8, 11. What is the next term and rule?

14; add 3 each time
14; multiply each term by 3
22; double the last term

choiceRationales: This response produces an inconsistent rule because the observed differences are all 3 rather than changing values.
Correct. Each consecutive difference is 3, so adding 3 to 11 gives the next term, 14. A constant additive rule generates the sequence.
This response would produce 6 as the second term, so it does not match the given sequence.
This response ignores the established additive relationship and produces a term outside the pattern.

items.prompt: Children count 24 craft sticks by making equal bundles. Which representation makes the tens-and-ones structure most visible?
choices: Twenty-four separate sticks in an unorganized pile
Four bundles of ten sticks and two single sticks
Two bundles of ten sticks and four single sticks
Two bundles of four sticks and ten single sticks
items.rationale: Bundling ten units creates a concrete base-ten representation: two tens account for 20 and four unbundled units account for 4.
choiceRationales: This response preserves the quantity but does not make the base-ten units or place-value structure readily visible.
This response represents 42 rather than 24 because the tens and ones have been reversed.
Correct. Bundling ten units creates a concrete base-ten representation: two tens account for 20 and four unbundled units account for 4.
This response contains 18 sticks and uses bundles of four, so it represents neither the quantity nor base-ten grouping.

items.prompt: Four children share one identical sandwich equally. Which share should each child receive?
choices: One half of the sandwich
One third of the sandwich
Four whole sandwiches
One fourth of the sandwich

choiceRationales: This response would create only two equal shares and would require two sandwiches for four children.
This response would create only three shares, leaving the number of equal parts inconsistent with the number of children.
This response confuses the number of children with the amount each child receives.

choiceRationales: Correct. Each of the three rows contains four buttons, so repeated addition uses three equal addends of 4 and totals 12.
This response adds the dimensions once and gives 7 rather than counting every object in the array.
This response represents three groups of 3, which totals 9 rather than the displayed 12 buttons.

items.prompt: Where should 37 be placed on a number line marked at 30 and 40 with ten equal intervals?

Seven intervals to the right of 30
Seven intervals to the left of 30
At the same point as 40

choiceRationales: This response locates 33 because it advances only three units from the labeled 30.
Correct. Ten equal intervals from 30 to 40 each represent one unit. Since 37 is 7 greater than 30, it is seven intervals to the right.

items.prompt: A child estimates 48 + 31 as 800. Which response best develops reasonableness?
choices: Accept 800 because estimates do not need to be close to the quantities.
Add a zero to every estimated sum regardless of the addends.
Compare 48 and 31 with 50 and 30, then use 50 + 30 = 80 as a benchmark.
Count the letters in the written number words instead of comparing magnitude.

choiceRationales: This response misstates estimation; a useful estimate remains reasonably close to the expected magnitude.
This response creates a place-value error rather than connecting the estimate to the given quantities.
Correct. Compatible benchmark numbers show that the sum should be near 80. This magnitude check reveals that 800 is about ten times too large.
This response uses an orthographic feature unrelated to numerical value or operation.

items.prompt: A child solves 42 − 18 by writing 42 − 10 = 32 and 32 − 8 = 24. Which explanation is accurate?
choices: The child changed subtraction into multiplication and found four groups of six.
The child subtracted 10 twice because 18 contains two tens.
The child used rounding, so 24 is only an estimate rather than the exact difference.
The child decomposed 18 into 10 and 8 and subtracted both parts while preserving the difference.
items.rationale: Decomposing the subtrahend into 10 and 8 permits two manageable subtraction steps. Because both parts total 18, the result 24 is exact.
choiceRationales: This response misidentifies the operation and invents groups that do not appear in the written strategy.
This response uses an incorrect place-value claim because 18 contains one ten and eight ones.
This response confuses decomposition with rounding; no quantity was replaced by an approximate benchmark.
Correct. Decomposing the subtrahend into 10 and 8 permits two manageable subtraction steps. Because both parts total 18, the result 24 is exact.

items.prompt: Children compare desk lengths using linking cubes. Which procedure produces a valid comparison?

Use large cubes for one desk and small cubes for the other, then compare the counts directly.
Leave spaces between cubes on one desk to make its unit count easier to see.
Begin measuring one desk several centimeters away from its edge.
items.rationale: Measurement comparisons require an equal unit iterated continuously from a common endpoint. Same-size cubes without gaps or overlaps preserve the meaning of the counts.
choiceRationales: Correct. Measurement comparisons require an equal unit iterated continuously from a common endpoint. Same-size cubes without gaps or overlaps preserve the meaning of the counts.
This response changes unit size, so the two numerical counts do not represent directly comparable lengths.
This response adds unmeasured space between units and inflates the apparent length.

8 cm
14 cm
3 cm

items.prompt: A picture graph uses one star to represent two books. A row has three full stars. How many books does the row represent?

5 books
6 books
8 books

choiceRationales: This response counts symbols but ignores that each symbol stands for two books.
This response combines the number of symbols and key value by addition instead of repeated groups.
Correct. Each symbol represents 2 books, so three symbols represent 3 × 2, or 6 books. The key must be applied to every symbol.

items.prompt: A bag contains only red counters. Which outcome is certain when one counter is drawn without looking?
choices: The counter will be blue.
The counter will be both red and blue.
The bag will contain more counters after the draw.
The counter will be red.
items.rationale: Because every possible counter in the bag is red, drawing a red counter has probability 1 and is a certain event. No other color is possible under the stated condition.
choiceRationales: This response is impossible under the stated contents because no blue counter is available.
This response assigns two mutually exclusive colors to one counter without any supporting condition.
This response confuses selecting an existing object with adding a new object to the bag.
Correct. Because every possible counter in the bag is red, drawing a red counter has probability 1 and is a certain event.

items.prompt: Two rows of six counters are equal. One row is spread farther apart, and a child says it now has more. Which action best assesses conservation of number?
choices: Have the child pair each counter in one row with one counter in the other and recount if needed.
Add a counter to the compact row so both rows look the same length.
Tell the child the answer without allowing any comparison of the sets.
Sort each row by counter color and compare only the darkest counters.
items.rationale: One-to-one correspondence demonstrates that spacing changed while the number of objects did not. Pairing separates quantity from visual length.
choiceRationales: Correct. One-to-one correspondence demonstrates that spacing changed while the number of objects did not. Pairing separates quantity from visual length.
This response changes the quantity and therefore prevents assessment of whether the original equal sets were conserved.
This response provides no observable reasoning or opportunity for the child to revise the spacing-based judgment.
This response introduces an irrelevant attribute and may exclude objects that still belong to each counted set.

items.prompt: A child sorts all triangles into a “point up” group and rejects a rotated triangle. Which response best addresses the misconception?
choices: Teach that a triangle must have one horizontal side at the bottom.
Rotate several examples and verify that each still has three straight sides and three vertices.
Rename the rotated example a diamond because orientation changes shape identity.
Compare only the colors of the examples and ignore their side structure.
items.rationale: Orientation is not a defining attribute of a triangle. Rotating varied examples while checking three sides and three vertices focuses attention on defining properties.
choiceRationales: This response turns a common prototype orientation into a false defining rule.
Correct. Orientation is not a defining attribute of a triangle. Rotating varied examples while checking three sides and three vertices focuses attention on defining properties.
This response misclassifies the figure because rigid rotation preserves side and vertex relationships.
This response directs attention to a nondefining attribute rather than the geometric structure.

items.prompt: Which object is best described as a rectangular prism?
choices: A flat rectangular card
A round dinner plate
A closed cereal box
A triangular road sign
items.rationale: A closed cereal box has six rectangular faces and three dimensions, which are defining features of a rectangular prism. It has length, width, and height.
choiceRationales: This response is a two-dimensional rectangle rather than a solid with depth and faces.
This response has a circular face and curved boundary rather than six rectangular faces.
Correct. A closed cereal box has six rectangular faces and three dimensions, which are defining features of a rectangular prism.
This response is a two-dimensional triangle and does not have the volume or face structure of a prism.

items.prompt: Two congruent right triangles are joined along their slanted sides. Which shape can they form?
choices: A circle
A sphere
A pentagon with unequal sides in every arrangement
A rectangle
items.rationale: Two congruent right triangles can compose a rectangle when corresponding hypotenuses are joined and the outer angles form four right angles.
choiceRationales: This response cannot be formed from straight-edged triangles because a circle requires a continuous curved boundary.
This response is three-dimensional and cannot result from joining two flat triangles in a plane.
This response makes an absolute claim contradicted by a valid four-sided rectangle composition.
Correct. Two congruent right triangles can compose a rectangle when corresponding hypotenuses are joined and the outer angles form four right angles.

items.prompt: On a north-up classroom map, the shelf is southeast of the rug. Which route moves from the rug to the shelf?
choices: Move diagonally down and to the right.
Move diagonally up and to the left.
Move straight up without changing horizontal position.
Move straight left without changing vertical position.
items.rationale: On a north-up map, southeast is represented by movement toward the bottom and right. The route therefore changes both vertical and horizontal position in the stated directions.
choiceRationales: Correct. On a north-up map, southeast is represented by movement toward the bottom and right. The route therefore changes both vertical and horizontal position in the stated directions.
This response describes northwest movement, the direction opposite the shelf’s stated location from the rug.
This response describes northward movement and omits the eastward component required to reach a southeast location.
This response describes westward movement and omits the southward component required to reach a southeast location.

items.prompt: A child says a square is not a rectangle. Which explanation is mathematically accurate?
choices: A square belongs in a separate category because its equal sides prevent it from satisfying the rectangle definition.
A square is a special rectangle because it has four right angles; it also has four equal sides.
A rectangle has two right angles and two other angles, whereas a square has four right angles.
Orientation determines the category; tilting the square creates the rectangle classification.
items.rationale: Hierarchical classification places every four-right-angle quadrilateral in the rectangle category. A square meets that definition and adds the property of four equal sides.
choiceRationales: This response uses an exclusive-category misconception: equal side lengths add a property but do not remove the four right angles required of rectangles.
Correct. Hierarchical classification places every four-right-angle quadrilateral in the rectangle category. A square meets that definition and adds the property of four equal sides.
This response states a false definition because a rectangle has four right angles rather than a mixture of right and nonright angles.
This response uses orientation as a defining attribute even though a rigid rotation does not change side or angle relationships.

items.prompt: Children propose two ways to solve 9 + 6. Which teacher question best promotes mathematical reasoning?
choices: “Which child finished first, regardless of whether the strategy is accurate?”
“Can everyone erase their method and copy the teacher’s steps exactly?”
“How does each strategy keep the total unchanged, and where can we see 15 in both representations?”
“Which representation uses the most colors, even if the quantities differ?”
items.rationale: Comparing how two strategies preserve quantity asks children to justify equivalence, connect representations, and attend to mathematical evidence.
choiceRationales: This response values speed rather than correctness, explanation, or structural relationships.
This response removes opportunities to analyze and connect valid strategies by imposing one procedure.
Correct. Comparing how two strategies preserve quantity asks children to justify equivalence, connect representations, and attend to mathematical evidence.
This response uses an aesthetic feature unrelated to whether the representations model the same sum.
